questions.question:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {ww^R | w in {a, b}^+}
L_2 = {a^n b^n | n >= 1}
Note that w^R is the reversal of the string w. Which of the following is/are TRUE?
A. L_1 and L_2 are regular.
B. L_1 and L_2 are context-free.
C. L_1 is regular and L_2 is context-free.
D. L_1 and L_2 are context-free but not regular.
questions.to_find: Which statements about the language classes of L_1 and L_2 are true.

understand.plain: L_1 is the set of all even-length palindromes over {a,b}. L_2 is the classic language of equal numbers of a's followed by b's. We need to determine whether each is regular or context-free or neither.

keywords.explain: Strings that are mirror images of themselves; ww^R always has even length and is a palindrome.
keywords.example: w=ab gives ww^R=abba; w=a gives aa

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a pushdown automaton (PDA); strictly more powerful than regular languages.
keywords.example: a^n b^n is the canonical CFL not accepted by any DFA

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a finite automaton; cannot count unboundedly.
keywords.example: a*b* is regular; a^n b^n is not

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='240' cy='100' rx='200' ry='65' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>All Languages</text><ellipse cx='240' cy='110' rx='160' ry='48' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Context-Free (L_1, L_2 live here)</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='118' rx='60' ry='28' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Regular</text><text x='350' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L_1, L_2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hierarchy diagram: Regular subset-of CFL subset-of All Languages. L_1 (even palindromes) and L_2 (a^n b^n) are both in CFL but outside Regular.

given.aim: Classify L_1 = {ww^R | w in {a,b}^+} and L_2 = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} as regular, CFL, or neither.

terms.meaning: Set of all even-length palindromes over {a,b} with at least 2 characters.
terms.connects: A PDA can push first half and match second half; it is CFL but not regular (pumping lemma).

terms.meaning: Equal number of a's followed by equal number of b's.
terms.connects: Classic CFL; not regular since a DFA cannot count unboundedly.

terms.meaning: Every regular language has a pumping length; strings longer than it can be pumped in the middle of any partition.
terms.example: For a^n b^n, pumping any prefix breaks the balance, so it is not regular.
terms.connects: Used to prove L_1 and L_2 are NOT regular.

given.plan: Apply pumping lemma to show neither L_1 nor L_2 is regular. Construct PDAs to confirm both are CFL. Evaluate each option.

steps.title: Is L_2 = a^n b^n regular?
steps.apply: Assume L_2 is regular with pumping length p. Take s = a^p b^p. For any split s = xyz with |xy| <= p and |y| >= 1, y consists entirely of a's. Pumping up gives a^(p+|y|) b^p, which is NOT in L_2. Contradiction. So L_2 is NOT regular.
steps.note: L_2 is the textbook example of a non-regular CFL.

steps.title: Is L_1 = {ww^R} regular?
steps.apply: Take s = a^p b b a^p (length 2p+2 > p). Any split with |xy| <= p has y = a^k for some k >= 1. Pumping gives a^(p+k) b b a^p which is NOT a palindrome of the form ww^R. Contradiction. So L_1 is NOT regular.
steps.note: Recognizing ww^R requires a PDA: push w, then match w^R.

steps.title: Are L_1 and L_2 context-free?
steps.apply: L_2: PDA pushes a's and pops on each b; accepts when stack is empty. L_1: PDA non-deterministically guesses the midpoint, pushes first half, then pops matching reversed second half. Both are CFLs.
steps.note: Both are standard CFL examples taught in every TOC course.

steps.title: Evaluate options
steps.apply:
A. FALSE — neither is regular.
B. TRUE — both are CFL.
C. FALSE — L_1 is also CFL (not regular).
D. TRUE — both are CFL but not regular.
steps.note: Options B and D are both correct; this is an MSQ.

solution.result: B and D are TRUE: both L_1 and L_2 are context-free but not regular.

questions.formula_note: Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages (to prove non-regularity); PDA construction (to confirm CFL membership).

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are CORRECT?
A. The intersection of two regular languages is regular.
B. The intersection of two context-free languages is context-free.
C. The intersection of two recursive languages is recursive.
D. The intersection of two recursively enumerable languages is recursively enumerable.
questions.to_find: Which closure properties under intersection hold for the given language classes.

understand.plain: This question tests closure properties of language classes. We need to check for each class whether the intersection of two languages in that class also belongs to the same class.

keywords.explain: A class L is closed under intersection if for any two languages A, B in L, A intersection B is also in L.
keywords.example: Regular languages: DFA for A intersect B can be built by product construction.

keywords.explain: Languages accepted by PDAs; NOT closed under intersection (e.g., {a^n b^n c^n} = {a^n b^n c* } intersect {a* b^n c^n}).
keywords.example: {a^n b^n c* } and {a* b^n c^n} are both CFL but their intersection {a^n b^n c^n} is not.

keywords.explain: Recursive = decidable (Turing machine always halts). RE = Turing-recognizable. Both are closed under intersection.
keywords.example: For recursive: simulate both TMs; if both accept then accept. For RE: same construction.

understand.visual_alt: Language hierarchy: Regular subset CFL subset Recursive subset RE. Regular, Recursive, and RE are closed under intersection; CFL is not.

given.aim: Determine which language classes are closed under intersection.

terms.meaning: Product construction on two DFAs gives a DFA for intersection.
terms.example: DFA for a* intersect *b gives DFA for a*b*... combined states.

terms.meaning: Counterexample: L1={a^n b^n c* } and L2={a* b^n c^n} are CFL; L1 intersect L2 = {a^n b^n c^n} is not CFL (proved by pumping lemma for CFL).
terms.example: {a^n b^n c^n} requires counting 3 equal segments, impossible for PDA.

terms.meaning: For recursive: build a 2-tape TM that simulates both; accepts iff both accept. For RE: same argument.
terms.example: If TM1 decides L1 and TM2 decides L2, then run both in sequence; accept if both accept.
terms.connects: Options C and D are TRUE.

given.plan: Check closure under intersection for each class using known closure properties and counterexamples.

steps.title: Regular languages (Option A)
steps.formula_raw: Product construction: Q = Q_1 x Q_2
steps.apply: Given DFA M1 for L1 and DFA M2 for L2, construct product DFA M with states Q1 x Q2, accepting only states where both components are accepting. M accepts exactly L1 intersection L2. So intersection of two regular languages is regular.
steps.note: Regular languages are closed under all Boolean operations (union, intersection, complement).

steps.title: Context-free languages (Option B)
steps.formula_raw: Counterexample: L1 intersect L2 = {a^n b^n c^n}
steps.apply: L1 = {a^n b^n c^k | n,k >= 0} is CFL (PDA counts a's and b's). L2 = {a^k b^n c^n | n,k >= 0} is CFL (PDA counts b's and c's). But L1 intersect L2 = {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 0} is NOT CFL (by pumping lemma for CFL). So CFL is NOT closed under intersection.
steps.note: CFL IS closed under intersection with a regular language (that's a different property).

steps.title: Recursive languages (Option C)
steps.formula_raw: TM1 decides L1, TM2 decides L2 => TM decides L1 intersect L2
steps.apply: Construct TM: on input w, run TM1(w); if reject then reject; else run TM2(w); accept iff TM2 accepts. Since both TMs always halt, the combined TM always halts. So intersection of two recursive languages is recursive.
steps.note: Recursive = decidable; closed under all Boolean operations.

steps.title: Recursively Enumerable languages (Option D)
steps.formula_raw: TM1 recognizes L1, TM2 recognizes L2 => TM recognizes L1 intersect L2
steps.apply: Construct TM: on input w, dovetail simulation of TM1 and TM2 (or run sequentially if guaranteed to halt). For w in L1 intersect L2, both eventually accept, so the combined TM accepts. So RE is closed under intersection.
steps.note: RE is closed under union and intersection but NOT complement.

solution.result: A, C, D are correct. CFL (option B) is NOT closed under intersection.

questions.formula_note: Closure properties of language classes: Regular closed under all Boolean ops; CFL not closed under intersection; Recursive and RE closed under intersection.

questions.question:
Let L be a regular language and M be a context-free language, both over the alphabet Sigma. Let L' and M' denote the complements of L and M respectively. Which of the following statements about the language L' union M is TRUE?
A. It is necessarily regular but not necessarily context-free.
B. It is necessarily context-free.
C. It is necessarily non-regular.
D. None of the above.
questions.to_find: The language class of L' union M where L is regular and M is CFL.

understand.plain: The complement of a regular language is regular. The union of a regular language and a CFL is a CFL. So L' (complement of regular) is regular, and L' union M is the union of a regular language and a CFL, which is always CFL.

keywords.explain: Regular languages are closed under complement; swapping accepting and non-accepting states in DFA gives DFA for complement.
keywords.example: Complement of a*b* over {a,b} includes strings like ba, abb, etc.

keywords.explain: CFL is closed under union with regular languages (and also under union with other CFLs). Result is always CFL.
keywords.example: a^n b^n union a*b* = CFL (the regular part doesn't push it out of CFL).

understand.visual_alt: L is regular => L' is regular. M is CFL. Regular subset of CFL, so L' is also CFL. Union of two CFLs is CFL. Therefore L' union M is CFL.

given.aim: Classify L' union M where L is regular and M is CFL.

terms.meaning: L is accepted by some DFA.
terms.connects: Complement L' is also regular (DFA complement construction).

terms.meaning: M is accepted by some PDA.
terms.connects: M is CFL; union with a regular language stays CFL.

terms.meaning: If L1 and L2 are CFL, then L1 union L2 is CFL.
terms.example: PDA for L1 union L2: non-deterministically choose which PDA to simulate.
terms.connects: Since L' is regular (hence CFL) and M is CFL, their union is CFL.

given.plan: L regular => L' regular => L' is CFL. M is CFL. CFL closed under union => L' union M is CFL. Check each option.

steps.title: Complement of L
steps.apply: L is regular. Regular languages are closed under complement. Therefore L' is also regular. Since every regular language is CFL, L' is also a CFL.
steps.note: DFA complement: flip accepting and non-accepting states.

steps.title: Union L' union M
steps.apply: L' is regular (hence CFL) and M is CFL. CFL is closed under union. Therefore L' union M is necessarily CFL.
steps.note: The union could be regular in some special cases (e.g., if M is regular), but it is NECESSARILY CFL in all cases.

steps.title: Check each option
steps.apply:
A. Not necessarily regular — M itself may not be regular (e.g., M = {a^n b^n}), and union with L' may not regularize it. FALSE.
B. Necessarily CFL — proven above. TRUE.
C. Not necessarily non-regular — if L and M are both regular, L' union M is regular. FALSE.
D. Not 'none of the above' since B is true. FALSE.
steps.note: B is the correct and precise answer.

questions.formula_note: Closure properties: Regular closed under complement; CFL closed under union; every regular language is also CFL.

questions.question:
The language {(0^P)^P | P is a prime} is:
A. regular
B. context-free but not regular
C. context-free but its complement is not context-free
D. not context-free
questions.to_find: The language class of {0^(p^2) | p is prime}.

understand.plain: The language consists of strings of 0s whose length is p^2 where p is a prime number. So lengths are 4, 9, 25, 49, 121, ... (squares of primes). We need to show this language is not context-free.

keywords.explain: Integers greater than 1 with no divisors other than 1 and themselves: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, ...
For any CFL L, there exists p such that any string s in L with |s| >= p can be split as s = uvwxy with |vwx| <= p, |vx| >= 1, and uv^i wx^i y in L for all i >= 0.
keywords.example: If pumping breaks the required structure (prime squares), the language is not CFL.

understand.visual_alt: Lengths of strings in L: 4 (=2^2), 9 (=3^2), 25 (=5^2), 49 (=7^2), 121 (=11^2). These are squares of primes, sparsely distributed among all integers.

given.aim: Show that {0^(p^2) | p prime} is not context-free by applying the CFL pumping lemma.

terms.meaning: All strings of 0s with length equal to the square of a prime.
terms.connects: If L were CFL, the pumping lemma would require all sufficiently pumped strings to stay in L.

terms.meaning: Every string in L longer than n can be pumped (length of vx increases by a fixed amount each pump).
terms.example: If s = 0^(p^2) and vx = 0^k, pumping gives 0^(p^2 + mk) for each m.
terms.connects: We need p^2 + mk to be prime-squared for all m >= 0, which is impossible.

terms.meaning: p^2 + mk for fixed k is an arithmetic progression; it contains non-prime-squares between consecutive prime squares.
terms.example: If p^2=9, k=2: 9, 11, 13, ... — 11 and 13 are not perfect squares of primes.
terms.connects: Shows the pumped string cannot always stay in L, so L is not CFL.

given.plan: Assume L is CFL, apply pumping lemma. Choose s = 0^(q^2) where q is prime and q^2 >= pumping length. Show that pumping produces strings not in L.

steps.title: Assume L is CFL and apply pumping lemma
steps.formula_raw: CFL Pumping Lemma: s = uvwxy, |vwx| <= n, |vx| >= 1, uv^i wx^i y in L for all i >= 0
steps.apply: Suppose L is CFL with pumping length n. Choose prime q such that q^2 > n, and let s = 0^(q^2). Then |s| = q^2 >= n, so s can be written as s = uvwxy with |vwx| <= n and |vx| >= k >= 1.
steps.note: The string has only one character (0), so vx = 0^k for some 1 <= k <= n.

steps.title: Pump and derive contradiction
steps.apply: Pumping gives strings of length q^2 + (i-1)k for varying i. For the language L, these lengths must all be squares of primes. Consider length q^2 + k (i=2). We need q^2 + k = r^2 for some prime r. So r^2 - q^2 = k, meaning (r-q)(r+q) = k. Since k <= n < q^2, this could hold for specific values. However, pumping ALL values of i must work: lengths q^2, q^2+k, q^2+2k, q^2+3k, ... form an arithmetic progression. By Dirichlet's theorem and density arguments, not all terms in this AP can be squares of primes. In fact, between q^2 and (q+1)^2 there are no perfect squares, so for large q the next prime-squared is (r^2) where r >> q, making the AP fail.
steps.note: More directly: if q^2 < q^2+k < (q+1)^2, then q^2+k is not a perfect square at all (let alone a prime square), contradicting membership in L.

steps.title: Conclude not CFL
steps.apply: Since pumping produces strings not in L, L is not CFL. Hence it is also not regular. Options A, B, C are all ruled out, making D correct.
steps.note: The language is actually context-sensitive (it can be recognized by a linear-bounded automaton).

solution.result: D. The language is not context-free.

questions.formula_note: CFL Pumping Lemma; density argument about squares of primes in arithmetic progressions.

questions.question:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {a^i b^j | i != j, i >= 0, j >= 0}
L_2 = {a^i b^j | i = 2j, i >= 0}
Which of the following is true?
A. L_1 is not a CFL but L_2 is.
B. L_1 and L_2 are both CFLs and L_2 is non-regular.
C. L_1 is not a CFL but L_2 is.
D. There is a 4-state PDA that accepts L_1, but there is no DPDA that accepts L_2.
questions.answer: B. L_1 and L_2 are both CFLs and L_2 is non-regular.
questions.to_find: Which statement correctly classifies L_1 and L_2 as CFL or non-CFL.

understand.plain: L_1 is the set of strings a^i b^j where the number of a's is not equal to the number of b's. L_2 is the set of strings a^i b^j where the number of a's is exactly twice the number of b's. We need to determine which is CFL.

keywords.explain: Strings of a's and b's (in order) where counts differ. Note L_1 = complement of {a^n b^n} intersected with a*b*, but CFL is not closed under complement.
keywords.example: ab (1a,2b: wait, that's i=1,j=2 so 1!=2 yes), aabb (2a,2b: 2=2 so NOT in L_1), a (1a,0b: in L_1)

keywords.explain: Strings where the number of a's is exactly twice the number of b's.

Complement of a DCFL is also DCFL. If L_1 were CFL, its complement a*b* minus L_1 = {a^n b^n | n >= 0} would need to be CFL (it is), but that's not the issue. Actually L_1 = {a^i b^j | i > j} union {a^i b^j | i < j}.
keywords.example: Both {a^i b^j | i > j} and {a^i b^j | i < j} are CFLs, so their union L_1 is CFL.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Classifying L_1 and L_2</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='200' height='120' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L_1 = {a^i b^j | i!=j}</text><text x='120' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>= {i>j} union {i<j}</text><text x='120' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>CFL (union of 2 CFLs)</text><text x='120' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Also DCFL</text><rect x='260' y='50' width='200' height='120' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='360' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L_2 = {a^i b^j | i=2j}</text><text x='360' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>PDA: push a, pop 2 a's</text><text x='360' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>CFL (actually regular!)</text><text x='360' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>DFA can track ratio</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: L_1 is the union of {i>j} and {i<j}, both CFLs. L_2 counts a's as twice b's, accepted by a simple PDA or even DFA tracking the difference.

given.aim: Classify L_1 = {a^i b^j | i != j} and L_2 = {a^i b^j | i = 2j} as CFL or not.

terms.meaning: Strings where count of a's differs from count of b's (i >= 0, j >= 0).
terms.connects: Can be written as union of two simpler CFLs.
PDA pushes 2 a's per b read; or DFA using modular counting.

terms.meaning: If L_A and L_B are CFL, then L_A union L_B is CFL.
terms.example: {i>j} is CFL (push a's, pop on b, accept if stack non-empty at end). {i<j} is CFL similarly.

given.plan: Decompose L_1 as union of two CFLs. Show L_2 is CFL (via PDA). Match correct option.

steps.title: Decompose and classify L_1
steps.formula_raw: L_1 = L_A union L_B where L_A = {a^i b^j | i > j}, L_B = {a^i b^j | i < j}
steps.apply:
L_A = {a^i b^j | i > j, i >= 0, j >= 0}: PDA pushes a's, pops one a per b, accepts if stack is non-empty after all b's. This is CFL.
L_B = {a^i b^j | i < j, i >= 0, j >= 0}: PDA pushes b's, pops one per a read at start, accepts if stack non-empty. This is CFL.
Since CFL is closed under union, L_1 = L_A union L_B is CFL.
steps.note: L_1 is actually DCFL (deterministic CFL) as well.

steps.title: Classify L_2
steps.formula_raw: PDA: push two a's per a read, pop two per b read
steps.apply: L_2 = {a^i b^j | i = 2j}: PDA reads a's and pushes them on the stack; for each b read, pops 2 a's. After all input, accept if stack is empty. This is a valid PDA, so L_2 is CFL. Note: L_2 is actually regular (DFA keeps count modulo 2 and tracks if we are in a-phase or b-phase).
steps.note: L_2 is simpler than L_1 in some sense — it's a linear constraint on i and j.

steps.title: Evaluate options
steps.apply:
A. 'L_1 is not a CFL but L_2 is' — FALSE: L_1 IS a CFL (shown above as union of two CFLs).
B. 'L_1 and L_2 are both CFLs and L_2 is non-regular' — Both are CFLs: TRUE. Is L_2 non-regular? L_2 = {a^i b^j | i=2j}: a DFA can handle this by tracking whether we are reading a's, then b's, and accept if #a = 2*#b. But #a and #b can be unbounded, so a finite automaton cannot verify i=2j in general. By pumping lemma for regular languages, assuming L_2 is regular with pumping length p, take s = a^(2p) b^p. Pumping y = a^k gives a^(2p+k) b^p which requires 2p+k = 2p (impossible for k>=1). So L_2 is NOT regular. Thus B is TRUE.
C. Same as A form, FALSE.
D. FALSE: L_2 also has a DPDA.
steps.note: Official GATE 2008 answer: B. Both L_1 and L_2 are CFLs; L_2 is non-regular.

solution.result: B. L_1 and L_2 are both context-free languages, and L_2 is non-regular.

questions.formula_note: CFL closure under union; PDA construction for ratio constraints; DCFL properties.
